title: Heat can help
body: A warm compress or hot water bottle on your lower abdomen may ease cramp discomfort.

title: Rest is productive
body: Your body is doing real work this week. Lighter activity and extra sleep are a good call.

title: Iron-rich foods
body: Leafy greens, lentils, and beans can help replenish iron lost during your period.

title: Pain relief options
body: Over-the-counter pain relief may help with cramps — your pharmacist can advise on what's suitable for you.

title: Stay hydrated
body: Blood loss increases your need for fluids. Warm drinks can also ease cramping.

title: Gentle movement can help
body: Light walking or gentle yoga may reduce cramp intensity for some people — listen to your body.

title: Bloating is common
body: Hormonal shifts cause water retention at the start of your period. It usually eases within a few days.

title: Fatigue is real
body: The drop in oestrogen and progesterone at menstruation is physically demanding. Fatigue now is not laziness.

title: Energy is building
body: Oestrogen rises during this phase — many people find focus and motivation easier right now.

title: Good time for new things
body: Starting a new project or learning something new tends to feel easier in the follicular phase.

title: Movement feels easier
body: Higher oestrogen supports muscle recovery — a good window for more intense activity if that works for you.

title: Support your gut
body: Fermented foods like yogurt and kimchi support the gut microbiome which influences hormone processing.

title: Skin often clears
body: Oestrogen tends to reduce sebum production in the follicular phase — many people notice clearer skin.

title: Social energy rises
body: Many people feel more confident and outgoing as oestrogen climbs. A good time to schedule things you have been putting off.

title: Peak energy window
body: Many people feel most social and energised around ovulation — a good time for presentations or important conversations.

title: Basal temperature rises
body: A slight rise in basal body temperature after ovulation is normal and confirms ovulation has occurred.

title: Stay hydrated
body: Hormonal shifts around ovulation can increase body temperature slightly. Extra water helps.

title: Notice your body
body: Some people feel mild one-sided pelvic discomfort around ovulation (mittelschmerz). It is common and usually brief.

title: Discharge changes are normal
body: Clear or stretchy cervical mucus changes around ovulation are a normal hormonal sign. If you notice changes in colour, smell, or texture at other times, it's worth mentioning to a doctor.

title: Communication feels easier
body: Verbal fluency and confidence often peak around ovulation. Difficult conversations may feel more manageable now.

title: Mood may shift
body: Progesterone rises then drops in the luteal phase. If your mood dips in the second half that is a recognised pattern.

title: Magnesium may help
body: Dark chocolate, nuts, and leafy greens are decent sources of magnesium, which some research links to reduced PMS symptoms.

title: Sleep changes are normal
body: Progesterone can affect sleep quality in the luteal phase. A cooler room and consistent sleep times help.

title: Reduce salt if bloated
body: Bloating is common in the late luteal phase. Reducing salty foods and increasing water can ease it.

title: Brain fog is hormonal
body: Difficulty concentrating in the luteal phase is linked to progesterone. It is not a reflection of your ability.

title: Cravings have a cause
body: Food cravings in the luteal phase are linked to serotonin fluctuations. Eating regularly helps stabilise them.

title: Severe symptoms deserve attention
body: If mood symptoms in the second half of your cycle significantly affect your daily life, it's worth discussing with your doctor — there are recognised patterns they can help you understand.

title: Breast tenderness is common
body: Progesterone causes breast tissue to swell in the luteal phase. A supportive bra and reducing caffeine can help.
